svg.title: Supplier Qualification Report — YAML template architecture
svg.desc: Six-section report anatomy for Option A showing field-level strategy assignments
svg.text: Report ID, issue date, review period, evaluator name
Supplier name, country, commodity category, Duns / VAT no.
Qualification scope statement (boilerplate per commodity type)
Scoring table — 6 criteria, score 1–5, weight, weighted score
Qualification verdict (Approved / Conditional / Rejected — from score)
Scorecard summary paragraph (scores + lowest criterion + verdict)
Performance metrics table (OTD %, defect rate, invoice accuracy)
Open NCR count, avg days to close, oldest open NCR age
SLA compliance flags (auto-derived — e.g. OTD < 90% → breach)
Performance narrative (trend language grounded in entered metrics)
Risk register table (risk item, likelihood 1–5, impact 1–5, owner)
Risk priority scores (likelihood × impact, per row)
Overall risk tier (Low / Medium / High — from max priority score)
Risk narrative (top 2 risks by priority, mitigation language)
CAR table (action item, owner, due date, status)
Approval conditions (if verdict = Conditional → conditions list)
CAR summary paragraph (count, priority items, target closure date)
Standard CAR follow-up clause (next review trigger boilerplate)
Executive summary paragraph (supplier, commodity, verdict, top risk)
Procurement recommendation paragraph (action + conditions if any)
Next review date (auto-computed from verdict — Approved 12m, Cond. 6m)
Approval signatory block, disclaimer, version footer
Beneq has no classifier — verdict was implicit. Here classifier is first-class:
score → verdict → conditions → narrative_llm all chain off it. That's the interesting orchestration.
